explanation: Answer: B | Pipe sizing considers: (1) required flow rate (volume per unit time); (2) allowable pressure drop (friction losses in the pipe and fittings); (3) fluid velocity (too low causes settling of solids; too high causes erosion, noise, and water hammer); (4) fluid properties (viscosity, density, corrosiveness); and (5) economics (larger pipe costs more but reduces operating costs). ASME/ANSI tables and Darcy-Weisbach equation guide sizing.

Answer: B | Vapor barriers (vapor retarders) are applied to the outer surface of insulation on cold piping and equipment to prevent atmospheric moisture vapor from migrating through the insulation to the cold surface, where it would condense and freeze, degrading insulation effectiveness and causing corrosion. Common materials include aluminum foil jacketing, PVC jacketing, and mastic coatings.

Answer: C | Pipe hangers and supports carry the weight of the pipe, its contents, and insulation, while controlling pipe movement. Fixed (rigid) supports prevent movement; guides allow axial movement only; spring hangers accommodate vertical thermal movement while supporting weight; and expansion anchors fix the pipe at specific points to direct expansion toward loops or joints. Improper support causes sagging, vibration, fatigue, and joint leakage.

Answer: D | Pipe schedule numbers (Sch 10, 40, 80, 160, XXS) indicate wall thickness for a given nominal pipe size (NPS). Higher schedule numbers indicate thicker walls and therefore higher pressure ratings. The outside diameter (OD) remains constant for a given NPS regardless of schedule; only the wall thickness (and therefore inside diameter and weight) changes. ASME B36.10 and B36.19 define pipe schedules.

Answer: A | A gasket fills the microscopic surface imperfections between two flange faces to create a pressure-tight seal. Gasket selection depends on fluid type, temperature, pressure, and flange face type. Common materials: spiral wound (high temperature/pressure service), ring-type joint (RTJ for highest pressure), compressed fiber (sheet gasket for low-pressure water/air), and full-face elastomeric (low-pressure, non-critical service).

Answer: B | Threaded pipe connections (NPT — National Pipe Taper) use a tapered thread that creates a seal by the wedging action of the mating tapers. PTFE tape or pipe dope (thread sealant) is applied to lubricate and fill thread imperfections. Threaded connections are used for smaller pipe sizes (typically NPS 2 inch and below), lower pressures, and where disassembly may be needed.

Answer: D | Flanged connections use mating flanges bolted together with a gasket between them to create a leak-tight, pressure-rated joint that can be disassembled for maintenance, inspection, or equipment removal. Flanges are specified by pressure class (150, 300, 600, 900, 1500, 2500 lb), face type (raised face, ring-type joint, flat face), and material. Bolt tightening sequence and torque are critical for even gasket seating.

Answer: A | Welded pipe joints (butt weld, socket weld, or fillet weld) provide permanent, strong connections with full pipe-wall integrity and no leak paths through mechanical joints. Required for high-pressure, high-temperature, toxic, or flammable service where joint reliability is critical. Welds must meet code requirements (ASME B31.1, B31.3) and are inspected by radiography, ultrasonic, or dye penetrant methods.

Answer: C | Pipe expansion loops (U-bends, Z-bends, or expansion joints) are intentionally designed flexible sections in piping systems that absorb the thermal expansion and contraction of pipes as they heat and cool during operation. Without them, constrained thermal expansion develops enormous forces on pipe supports, equipment nozzles, and structural attachments — causing piping failures and equipment damage.

Answer: B | Expansion joints (bellows, ball joints, slip joints, or rubber flexible couplings) in piping systems absorb thermal expansion and contraction, vibration, and minor misalignment between piping sections. Unlike expansion loops (which use the flexibility of the pipe itself), expansion joints are dedicated components that compress or extend. They must be properly guided and anchored to direct movement as designed.

option_b: Temperature control, energy conservation, personnel protection
explanation: Answer: B | Pipe insulation serves multiple purposes: conserves energy by reducing heat loss (hot piping) or heat gain (cold piping); maintains process fluid temperature within required limits; prevents condensation on cold piping (condensation control); protects personnel from contact with hot or cold surfaces (personnel protection, required above 60°C per most codes); and reduces thermal stress in piping systems.
